name: West Virginia
examName: West Virginia Real Estate Salesperson Exam

examFacts.notes: Administered by Pearson VUE for the West Virginia Real Estate Commission.

intro:
The West Virginia real estate salesperson exam is tougher than most first-time candidates expect. The West Virginia Real Estate Commission splits it into an 80-question national portion and a 50-question state portion, and you get 210 minutes total to clear both. You need 70% on each portion separately, so a strong national score won't rescue a weak state score. West Virginia layers its own license law on top of the national principles, and that West Virginia-specific material is exactly where unprepared candidates lose points.

State-specific prep matters more here than almost anywhere else. West Virginia tests the duties and powers of the Real Estate Commission, the statutes that govern licensee conduct, the state's agency-disclosure rules, trust-account requirements, and West Virginia's own fair housing protections. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need West Virginia-specific practice questions.

Below are 20 free West Virginia real estate test questions and answers from RealReady's full West Virginia bank. Each one tests a specific topic with the correct answer and a plain-English explanation under the card. Read each question, commit to an answer, then reveal it. Treat it like a real Pearson VUE practice test, and the ones you miss show you exactly what to study next.

Get the RealReady app on iPhone, iPad, or Android for the rest of the West Virginia question bank. The full app includes short articles that walk you through the why behind each topic, detailed explanations on every question, a missed-question drill mode, and progress tracking that shows your per-category accuracy.

faq.question: How many questions are on the West Virginia real estate exam?
faq.answer: The West Virginia salesperson exam has 130 multiple-choice questions, split into an 80-question national portion and a 50-question state portion. You get 210 minutes in total, and you need 70% on each portion to pass.

faq.question: What's the passing score for the West Virginia real estate exam?
faq.answer: You need 70% on each portion separately. The national and state sections are scored independently, so you have to clear both. You receive a score report marked pass or fail at the test center.

faq.question: How much does the West Virginia real estate exam cost?
faq.answer: The examination fee is $59 per exam part, paid to Pearson VUE when you reserve your seat. Because the salesperson exam has a national part and a state part, plan for that fee on each part you need to take.

faq.question: How long is the West Virginia real estate exam?
faq.answer: You get 150 minutes for the 80-question national portion and 60 minutes for the 50-question state portion, 210 minutes in all. That is a little over a minute per question, so a steady pace matters.

faq.question: Is the West Virginia real estate exam hard?
faq.answer: It is challenging, especially the state portion. Many candidates handle the national real estate principles fine but underestimate how much West Virginia-specific law the state section covers across its 50 questions. Focused state prep is what separates first-time passers from retakes.

faq.question: What's on the West Virginia real estate salesperson exam?
faq.answer: The national portion covers property ownership, land use controls, valuation, financing, agency, property disclosures, contracts, leasing and property management, transfer of title, the practice of real estate, and real estate calculations. The state portion focuses on the duties and powers of the West Virginia Real Estate Commission, the statutes that govern licensee activity, agency-disclosure rules, broker trust-account requirements, and West Virginia fair housing law.

faq.question: What's the best way to prepare for the West Virginia real estate exam?
faq.answer: Practice with West Virginia-specific questions until you score above 80% consistently, then keep drilling the topics you miss most. Review every explanation, not just the ones you get wrong, and use practice tests that mirror the real exam's split national-and-state format and difficulty.
